texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD), often integrated with crystallization (MDC), is an attractive treatment option for shale gas produced water (SGPW) because of its ability to handle high salinity as well as the inherent geothermal heat available to this process. To evaluate the feasibility of applying MD process for SGPW treatment, membrane fouling and wetting, which are barrier to its practical application, were systematically examined by varying organic and inorganic constituents, simulating the SGPW from Marcellus shale (USA). The liquid entry pressure (LEP) was first measured to predict the possibility of wetting by the chemical constituents of SGPW, and then a series of lab-scale MD and MDC experiments were performed to elucidate membrane wetting mechanisms. The results revealed that membrane wetting became more pronounced in the presence of oil and grease. The inorganic scaling induced by multivalent ions, such as barium and calcium in SGPW, also enhanced membrane wettability and led to poor permeate water quality. By integrating with crystallization, scalant loading was reduced properly and thus membrane wetting was mitigated effectively. As a result, adopting this MDC process increased total recovery up to 62.5%. Our experimental observations demonstrated that MD could be sustainably operated for SGPW treatment through optimized crystallization for scaling removal as well as effective pre-treatment for organic removal.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Shale gas is an emerging energy source with the potential to prolong the use of fossil fuel by several decades. Traditionally, this natural gas, trapped within shale formations, had been categorized as unconventional natural gas due to the poor economic feasibility of its extraction. However, with advancing shale gas extraction techniques including horizontal drilling and hydraulic fracturing, estimated technically recoverable global gas reserves have increased by over 40% since 2010 [1,2].
texts.text: Shale gas is an emerging energy source with the potential to prolong the use of fossil fuel by several decades. Traditionally, this natural gas, trapped within shale formations, had been categorized as unconventional natural gas due to the poor economic feasibility of its extraction. However, with advancing shale gas extraction techniques including horizontal drilling and hydraulic fracturing, estimated technically recoverable global gas reserves have increased by over 40% since 2010 [1,2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The extraction of shale gas using these new technologies unfortunately generates substantial aqueous waste, approximately 8000 -27,000 m 3 [3,4], which is collectively known as ' shale gas produced water ' (SGPW). The quantity and quality of SGPW produced vary spatially and temporally [2]. Mineral and organic constituents present in the shale formations dissolve into SGPW, creating a brine solution that includes high concentrations of salts, metals, oils, greases, and organic compounds [5]. Thus, when trying to simultaneously preserve the economic feasibility of shale gas development and ensure the protection of human health and the environment, SGPW management is one of the biggest challenges [2,4 -7].
texts.text: The extraction of shale gas using these new technologies unfortunately generates substantial aqueous waste, approximately 8000 -27,000 m 3 [3,4], which is collectively known as ' shale gas produced water ' (SGPW). The quantity and quality of SGPW produced vary spatially and temporally [2]. Mineral and organic constituents present in the shale formations dissolve into SGPW, creating a brine solution that includes high concentrations of salts, metals, oils, greases, and organic compounds [5]. Thus, when trying to simultaneously preserve the economic feasibility of shale gas development and ensure the protection of human health and the environment, SGPW management is one of the biggest challenges [2,4 -7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For SGPW management, there are two options, injection into underground disposal wells and internal reuse for shale gas well development [2,8]. The vast majority of SGPW is injected into underground wells [9]. However, underground injection is not only associated with environmental risk but also unavailable in the areas lacking the appropriate geology for injection well sites. Thus, in shale gas operation, a recent trend has been the increase of the proportion of SGPW internally reused. Nevertheless, the reuse strategy is not a perfect one since it is just a temporary solution. Furthermore, if SGPW volumes exceed internal reuse requirements, more comprehensive options are necessary, particularly external reuse which often demands SGPW desalination [2].
texts.text: For SGPW management, there are two options, injection into underground disposal wells and internal reuse for shale gas well development [2,8]. The vast majority of SGPW is injected into underground wells [9]. However, underground injection is not only associated with environmental risk but also unavailable in the areas lacking the appropriate geology for injection well sites. Thus, in shale gas operation, a recent trend has been the increase of the proportion of SGPW internally reused. Nevertheless, the reuse strategy is not a perfect one since it is just a temporary solution. Furthermore, if SGPW volumes exceed internal reuse requirements, more comprehensive options are necessary, particularly external reuse which often demands SGPW desalination [2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various physical, chemical, and biological treatment technologies are proposed for the desalination of SGPW. However, the high cost, use of toxic chemicals and large footprint are the main drawbacks of these conventional methods [9,10]. In contrast, membrane processes are characterized by the low susceptibility of permeate quality to feed conditions, the limited use of chemical additives, and a low cost [9]. For such membrane processes to be suitable technologies for SGPW treatment, they must be capable of desalinating high-salinity feed waters, have a low propensity for fouling, and be modular and scalable for on-site treatment [2]. Thus, membrane distillation (MD) is emerging as an SGPW treatment option [11].
texts.text: Various physical, chemical, and biological treatment technologies are proposed for the desalination of SGPW. However, the high cost, use of toxic chemicals and large footprint are the main drawbacks of these conventional methods [9,10]. In contrast, membrane processes are characterized by the low susceptibility of permeate quality to feed conditions, the limited use of chemical additives, and a low cost [9]. For such membrane processes to be suitable technologies for SGPW treatment, they must be capable of desalinating high-salinity feed waters, have a low propensity for fouling, and be modular and scalable for on-site treatment [2]. Thus, membrane distillation (MD) is emerging as an SGPW treatment option [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD is a separation process using micro-porous hydrophobic membranes which utilize low-grade heat to drive the diffusion of vapor molecules under a vapor pressure difference between feed and permeate across the membrane [12]. This membrane process is technologically attractive and inherently suitable for SGPW treatment. The primary drivers of the MD process for SGPW management can be summarized as follows: (1) MD is less sensitive to feed salinity [13 -15] (2) MD can be operated with geothermal heat that SGPW contains (3) MD insures almost complete salt rejection [9,16,17], and (4) MD exhibits a lower fouling propensity than other pressure-driven membrane processes [14 -16,18 -20].
texts.text: MD is a separation process using micro-porous hydrophobic membranes which utilize low-grade heat to drive the diffusion of vapor molecules under a vapor pressure difference between feed and permeate across the membrane [12]. This membrane process is technologically attractive and inherently suitable for SGPW treatment. The primary drivers of the MD process for SGPW management can be summarized as follows: (1) MD is less sensitive to feed salinity [13 -15] (2) MD can be operated with geothermal heat that SGPW contains (3) MD insures almost complete salt rejection [9,16,17], and (4) MD exhibits a lower fouling propensity than other pressure-driven membrane processes [14 -16,18 -20].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although MD has lower fouling propensity, membrane fouling and wetting result in deterioration of water productivity and quality, particularly in high recovery conditions [13,18,19,21 -23]. Also, certain feed components in SGPW, such as oil and grease, instigate membrane wetting through change in feed solution surface tension [14,18,22,24]. Thus, MD membrane fouling and wetting have been studied extensively [23,25 -28]. Furthermore, hybrid MD systems such as MD integrated with crystallization (MDC) have been employed for higher total process recovery by mitigating membrane fouling and wetting [29 -31]. Especially the hybrid system integrating MD with crystallization could offer a potential solution to the brine disposal problem by enabling nearly complete water recovery [31].

Especially the hybrid system integrating MD with crystallization could offer a potential solution to the brine disposal problem by enabling nearly complete water recovery [31].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, these studies of MD and MDC treating high salinity solutions such as SGPW have been confined to simple process performance evaluation, particularly salt rejection and water productivity [2,10,17, 32 -35]. Thus, a more fundamental approach is required to elucidate and overcome the main operational problems such as membrane fouling and wetting in the application of MD and MDC to SGPW treatment.

Thus, a more fundamental approach is required to elucidate and overcome the main operational problems such as membrane fouling and wetting in the application of MD and MDC to SGPW treatment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective of this paper is to investigate the effect of the chemical composition of SGPW on membrane fouling and wetting during MD and MDC processes. Membrane wetting potential was first estimated by measuring liquid entry pressure (LEP), known as the indicator of wettability. Then, lab-scale MD and MDC experiments were performed to systematically measure actual membrane fouling and wetting. Based on these results, the mechanisms of membrane fouling and wetting caused by the organic and inorganic constituents of SGPW were elucidated and delineated. Furthermore, the technological applicability of MD and MDC to SGPW treatment was evaluated.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Shale gas produced water (SGPW)
texts.text: 2.1. Shale gas produced water (SGPW)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Shale gas produced water (SGPW) is the water that returns to the surface after the hydraulic fracturing process is completed [4,5,36]. Flowback of the fracturing fluid occurs over a few days to a few weeks after hydraulic fracturing, depending on the geology. SGPW flowback is at its highest on the first day, and then diminishes over time. A typical initial rate may be as high as 1000 m 3 /d [37]. This brine solution typically contains high concentrations of salts, metals, oils, greases, and soluble organic compounds, both volatile and semi-volatile.
texts.text: Shale gas produced water (SGPW) is the water that returns to the surface after the hydraulic fracturing process is completed [4,5,36]. Flowback of the fracturing fluid occurs over a few days to a few weeks after hydraulic fracturing, depending on the geology. SGPW flowback is at its highest on the first day, and then diminishes over time. A typical initial rate may be as high as 1000 m 3 /d [37]. This brine solution typically contains high concentrations of salts, metals, oils, greases, and soluble organic compounds, both volatile and semi-volatile.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, SGPW was made synthetically according to Table 1. Chemicals were selected based on the typical range of constituents in the SGPW from development in the Marcellus shale, the largest shale gas reservoir in the USA [5]. The chemicals used, all from Sigma Aldrich, were NaBr, BaCl2, Na2SO4. CaCl2, NaCl, and oil and grease (QC3083). Synthetic SGPWs of modified composition were also created by substituting either calcium or barium with sodium while maintaining the same total dissolved solids (TDS) condition.

texts.orig: 2.2. MD membrane
texts.text: 2.2. MD membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A flat-sheet membrane (FGLP14250, Millipore), made of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) polymer and supported by a polyethylene (PE) net, was employed. The principal characteristics of the membrane as specified by the manufacturer were 0.22 μ m average pore size, 150 μ mthickness and 85% porosity. In addition, the maximum operating temperature was 130 °C, the air flow rate was 5 L/min cm 2 , the bubble point with 23 °C air and with methanol was ≥ 1.0 bar, the gravimetric extractable was 0.5%, and the water flow rate was 24 mL/ min cm 2 .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Liquid entry pressure (LEP) measurements
texts.text: 2.3. Liquid entry pressure (LEP) measurements

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The equipment, as depicted in Fig. 1, was specially designed to measure the LEP of MD membranes. The LEP is the pressure at which the first drop of the feed solution appears on the permeate side [38,39]. The system was composed of a reservoir, a rubber piston, a digital pressure gauge, and a pressure regulator. The wetting liquid chamber was filled with feed solutions of synthetic SGPW. The reservoir volume was 20 mL after excluding the piston volume. A membrane sample 47 mm in diameter and 17 cm 2 in effective surface area was placed above the liquid chamber, with support material above the membrane to provide mechanical strength. Nitrogen gas (99.99%) was used to generate the applied pressure which was controlled by a precision pressure regulator (HARRIS® MODEL 896). A digital pressure gauge (range, 0 to 6 bar; supplied by SMC®) measured the applied pressure relative to the atmospheric pressure.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. Membrane distillation (MD) with crystallization (MDC) experiments
texts.text: 2.4. Membrane distillation (MD) with crystallization (MDC) experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aseries of MD and MDC experiments were conducted using a labscale direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) integrated with crystallization (Fig. 2). The membrane cell was made of acrylic plastic to minimize heat loss to the surroundings and designed to hold a flat sheet membrane without support. The effective membrane area was 20 cm 2 . Feed solution was continuously recirculated from a feed reservoir to the MD cell and then a Teflon tube encasing a heating element before returning back to the feed reservoir. The cross-flow velocity of both solutions was fixed at 25 cm/s. 4 L of feed solution was prepared and maintained at typical SGPW temperature (i.e., 60 ± 0.5 °C) while the permeate solution was cold deionized water maintained at 20 ± 0.5 °C. Temperature sensors were placed immediately before the inlet and after the outlet of the feed to the membrane cell (T-1 and T-2 in Fig. 2). Likewise, temperature sensors were also installed immediately before the inlet and after the outlet of the distillation semi-cell (T-3 and T-4 in Fig. 2). The cold fluid temperature of the permeate solutions was regulated using a stainless steel heat exchange coil directly immersed in the permeate reservoir. The permeate solution was circulated from the permeate reservoir into the membrane semi-cell and back again. The reservoir collecting the condensing permeate water was continuously weighed on an analytical balance. The electrical conductivity of the permeate water was also continuously monitored using a conductivity meter (HACH® HQ40d).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To integrate MD with crystallization and create the MDC setup, the DCMD unit was combined with a crystallizer (Fig. 2). The crystallizer had an internal stirrer, and a constant temperature of 40 ± 1.0 °C maintained by a cooling jacket through which cool water was circulated. Crystals formed in the crystallizer were sampled and characterized. Other operating conditions, such as cross-flow velocity and temperature, were the same as the MD experiments.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5. Characterization of formed scales and crystals
texts.text: 2.5. Characterization of formed scales and crystals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To elucidate membrane wetting mechanisms, membrane coupons sampled after each MD and MDC experiment were analysed by Scanning Electron Microscopy with Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) and X-ray Powder Diffraction (XRD). The coupons were simply dried in a desiccator for 1 day before being analysed. The devices used for SEM-EDS and XRD were Quanta 250 FEG (FEI) and X-TOOL (ULVAC-PHI), respectively. After each MD and MDC experiment, SEMEDSanalysis was carried out first to identify the scales on the membrane surface. In addition, the XRD analysis was conducted on a powder of the crystals formed in and sampled from the crystallizer with concentrated feed solution during MDC experiments. Then, sampled solution was filtered by 0.45 μ mfilter and dried. The X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded for the scan angles (2 θ ) from 10 to 80° to determine barium carbonate and calcium carbonate.
texts.text: To elucidate membrane wetting mechanisms, membrane coupons sampled after each MD and MDC experiment were analysed by Scanning Electron Microscopy with Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) and X-ray Powder Diffraction (XRD). The coupons were simply dried in a desiccator for 1 day before being analysed. The devices used for SEM-EDS and XRD were Quanta 250 FEG (FEI) and X-TOOL (ULVAC-PHI), respectively. After each MD and MDC experiment, SEMEDSanalysis was carried out first to identify the scales on the membrane surface. In addition, the XRD analysis was conducted on a powder of the crystals formed in and sampled from the crystallizer with concentrated feed solution during MDC experiments. Then, sampled solution was filtered by 0.45 μ mfilter and dried. The X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded for the scan angles (2 θ ) from 10 to 80° to determine barium carbonate and calcium carbonate.

texts.orig: To further investigate the influence of oil and grease on membrane wetting, additional LEP measurements were conducted. As shown in Fig. 4, while up to oil and grease concentrations of 100 mg/L LEP remained around 3 bar and no significance decrease in LEP occurred, it decreased sharply with oil and grease concentrations of 150 mg/L or over. At 250 mg/L, a spontaneous wetting occurred with low LEP values indicating an aggravation of the hydrophobicity of the MD membrane. Overall, LEP decreased with oil and grease concentration. texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a result, oil and grease could degrade the hydrophobicity of the MDmembrane, and be a key factor in membrane wetting. Considering the unchanged water flux and premature increase in permeate conductivity with oil and grease, oil and grease do not influence the clogging of membranepores related to membrane permeability, but rather coat the inner side of the membrane pore channels and thus allow high salinity water molecules to pass through them in place of pure water vapor. Consequently, it is revealed that the residual oil and grease in SGPW induce more severe membrane wetting, and thus a pretreatment process to remove them is required to successfully apply the MD process to SGPW treatment.

texts.orig: Tofurther understand the membrane wetting derived from inorganic scaling with SGPW, an SEM-EDS analysis of wetted membrane was performed after each MD experiment. SEM images of the scales formed during DCMD processing of modified SGPW1 appeared to be spherical (Fig. 6a). The shape of the scales was similar to the general shape of calcium carbonate scales [44], and a major component of the scales was shown to be calcium based scales by EDS elemental analysis. In the case of modi fi ed SGPW2 (Fig. 6b), the shape of the scales was needle-like, suggestive of barium carbonate [45]. EDS elemental analysis showed that the needle-like scales were indeed barium based scales such as barium carbonate. In case of modified SGPWs 1 and 2 which had the multivalent ions barium and calcium, respectively, replaced by sodium, EDS elemental analysis also detected sodium scales. In addition, Fig. 6c showed that the scales formed in MD experiments with unmodified SGPW were a mixture of needle-like scales, the more dominant spherical scales, and no sodium scales. The scales were identified, respectively, as barium based scales (i.e., barium carbonate) and calcium based crystals (i.e., calcium carbonate) by EDS elemental analysis. From the results of the SEMEDS analysis, it can be concluded that multivalent ions are major factor because of their scaling dominance, despite their tiny concentration in SGPW (Table 1), and that calcium is more problematic than barium for the treatment of SGPW by MD. Although multivalent ions were perfectly removed from SGPW, the total recovery of the MD process was still limited to around 40% with proper permeate water quality due to scales of monovalent ions such as the sodium scales (e.g., sodium chloride).

texts.orig: Prediction of potential scales was conducted by saturation index (SI) calculation to prepare for the performance degradation problems induced by inorganic scaling under high recovery conditions. The concentrations of SGPW adopted by the SI calculation were those corresponding to the initiation of the conductivity increase in each MD experiment. As shown in Fig. 5, the increase in conductivity was first observed at a permeate volumeof 1000 mL in the MD experiment using unmodified SGPW, while in the experiments using the modified SGPWs 1 and 2, the same phenomenon was observed at an approximate permeate volume of 1400 mL. As depicted in Table 2, in the case of unmodified SGPW, the SI values of bariumcarbonate(Witherite), barium sulfate (Barite), and calcium carbonate (Aragaonite, Calcite) were positive, implying that they may be crystallized. In the case of modified SGPW1, the SI values of calcium carbonate were positive, while in the case of modified SGPW2, the SI values of barium carbonate and barium sulfate were positive. The substances presenting positive SI values were exactly the same as the scale components detected by the SEM-EDS analysis in the MD experiments, except for barium sulfate, which was at a small concentration in SGPW. In addition, although the SI values of sodium scales (Halite) increased with the removal of the multivalent ions barium and calcium, the SI value of sodium scales was still negative (Table 2), a finding supported by their lack of detection in the SEM-EDS analysis. However, sodium scales could crystallize on the membrane surface considering the concentration and temperature

texts.orig: 3.3. MD integrated with crystallization (MDC) for inorganic scaling mitigation
texts.text: 3.3. MD integrated with crystallization (MDC) for inorganic scaling mitigation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Tomitigate inorganic scaling, a single MD process was integrated with crystallization. These MDC experiments were conducted under the same operating conditions in terms of temperature, cross-flow velocity, and the chemical compositions of synthetic SGPW as the MD experiments. As shown in Fig. 7, during the MDC experiments the water flux decline and the conductivity increase were substantially delayed compared to the single MDexperiments. Due to the reduction of SGPW ionic concentration by forced crystallization in the crystallizer, the inorganic scalant loading of the MDC system could be decreased to such an extent that the MDC process could continue for much longer without membrane wetting or inorganic scaling. This allowed the total recovery of the MDC process to increase by 62.5%, or be twice that of the single MD process, while still retaining proper permeate water quality.

texts.orig: The flux decline caused by inorganic scaling was explained by some tiny crystals formed in the crystallizer which also crystallized on the membrane surface due to concentration polarization inducing the super-saturation of the scales near the membrane surface [31]. Although potential scales including the multivalent ions causing much of the inorganic scaling were effectively removed by forced crystallization in the crystallizer, deposition and crystallization on the membrane surface still reduced the performance of the MDC process. Thus, further research on separating crystals from feed solutions and the control of chemical solubility at the membrane surface and crystallizer by optimizing operating conditions such as temperature and cross-flow velocity are required to maximize the performance of SGPW treatment by MDC. Nevertheless, it is obvious that the MDC process is a promising alternative SGPW treatment option which can help reduce brine disposal by increasing total recovery from SGPW in the shale gas industry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, membrane fouling and wetting by shale gas produced water (SGPW) was fundamentally investigated to assess the applicability of membrane distillation (MD) integrated with crystallization (MDC) to SGPW treatment. The main conclusions drawn from this study are summarized as follows:
texts.text: In this work, membrane fouling and wetting by shale gas produced water (SGPW) was fundamentally investigated to assess the applicability of membrane distillation (MD) integrated with crystallization (MDC) to SGPW treatment. The main conclusions drawn from this study are summarized as follows:

texts.orig: · With no organics present in SGPW, the water flux of MD process was significantly reduced by inorganic scaling such as by barium and calcium-based scales. Inorganic scaling also initiated membrane wetting which manifested as the deterioration of permeate water quality. As a result, the total recovery of the MD process still remained at the low level of 37.5%. Thus, based on the modeling of anticipating potential scales, it was proposed to integrate MD process with crystallization.

Inorganic scaling also initiated membrane wetting which manifested as the deterioration of permeate water quality. As a result, the total recovery of the MD process still remained at the low level of 37.5%. texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · MDCprocesscould effectively mitigate the occurrence of inorganic scaling in the MD process, and maintain the stability of water production performance. The total recovery by the MDC process represented a performance increase of 62.5%. Nevertheless, further research on the MDC process is needed for its optimization.

The total recovery by the MDC process represented a performance increase of 62.5%. Nevertheless, further research on the MDC process is needed for its optimization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite several advantages of MD and MDC processes for SGPW management, its technological feasibility is largely determined by operational obstacles: membrane fouling and wetting. Our study demonstrated that membrane fouling and wetting are greatly affected by various potential foulants in SGPW. Therefore, the practical application of MD process to SGPW treatment could be facilitated by employing proper fouling and wetting control strategies including appropriate pretreatments as well as optimized crystallization.
